speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of the interview with Elizabeth Etinger, on October the 11th, 1996. You were talking before about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to say a few words about the club where we all-- why Germans went away. You see, if you take our logic, they just thought that we all are lunatics. We are not normal people. There's nothing to get out of us. We were screaming girls, running like idiots, and they just decided to give up on us. Because they couldn't get out nothing. So that's how we were left alone. But we were frightened that they will come back. So what we did, straight away, we start to run. And it was not only me and my sister and mother. There was a few families. We all started to go in all different directions, just run away from this club. We did-- we were sure they might come back. And we won't be as lucky again. So we walked. We walked a few kilometers. In all-- about maybe 60 maybe 70 kilometers away, we stop in one of those little village I would say, [NON-ENGLISH], but it's called in Russia, [NON-ENGLISH], because this was a Cossack's place. And Cossacks have a very bad reputation. They were the worst anti-Semites you can get. They were the ones that we were mostly afraid. Because Germans wouldn't know who is a Jew, who is not Jew. But Cossacks, they can smell kilometers where a Jew. They pointed to all Jews. They said, this is the Jew. And then Germans took the action. They took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, and we stayed there very little, because you had a-- a few a suitcases on this sort of wagon like, we found somewhere, because it was hard to-- to carry. All of a sudden, we heard, halt! So we stopped. And behind us was a German with a gun. So he come to this, and asks, what is that? We said, this is our things.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, go [POINTS] further up. We want to see. So we started to open these-- our things. And we were lucky. Because we wouldn't live long. And back there starting shooting and screaming. So this chap that had us under a gun, ran down there. And when we saw he ran, we start to run. We left everything. We went in the little thing, whatever we had on us, we run. And likely he never come back after us. We left these whole things, the suitcase and whatever we had, and ran away. From now on, we were really on the run, run, and run, and run. We come [SIGHS] to a place. We was asking for piece of bread. So we helped the Cossacks to do some cutting grass. Some-- there was a lot of sunflowers. So we-- we cut the sunflowers, and then we we did get some of sunflower seeds. That was our meal for the day. And we didn't stop in one particular-- we were-- wherever we stopped to get something to eat, we work for it to get something to eat. And till we come to another [NON-ENGLISH], Nikolaevka. And what happened, while we were working we saw a shed, practically a shed. And we said, look. We stay here. We come there. It was empty, nobody around. So me, my sister, and my mother, we went inside and stayed inside. And from there on, I said, my mother to stay there, not to go nowhere. Because we were frightened any time Germans will come, and my sister, you know. But I was going to look for something to eat. So I went to one place where it was [NON-ENGLISH], and they used to do oil from-- from sunflower, sunflower oil. To make the oil, you have to beat with the special thing on the seeds to make them-- how you call-- soft. And get oil out of it. And for this I usually get a whole bucket of sunflower seeds. That was nice. Now for many, many days we ate only sunflower seeds. But we survived. It was very filling. And we couldn't feel hunger after we ate that. I remember like-- there was not far a sort of-- not river, but a stream. We used to go there and get some water. And there, because nobody was around, mom went with me. And I remember like now, and she was sitting there, poor thing, [SOBBING] telling me if we have been in Palestine, we wouldn't have it. And I look at her. And I couldn't believe the words she said. See, my mother was not a Zionist. She was a Jew, with bone and blood. She was, remember Palestine. She said, if we would have been in Palestine, we wouldn't have to go through this whole thing. But that's not all. Then they start to come in, people around. And said, it was not far from this little town. And in this-- and this town start to-- people start to come, and telling us, there is a sign, all Jews has to come to this place to register.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: no, Nikolaevka, Nikolaevka. And we-- and when we heard that-- they-- they-- they were asking us for documents. But when we ran from there, [POINTING] from the German with the gun, we throw out all our documents. We didn't have a thing left. And when they ask us for documents, we said we lost them all. Because the bomb was, and things like this, we lost them all. When these people start to decide that all Jews have to go in this place, and they sent one person from-- from the town to ask us whether we're Jews. So we said we're not. So they wanted documents. And I said, we haven't got documents. All documents is we lost. He said, you have to-- to have documents because there was probably somebody pointed to us. I don't know. But he said, if you don't produce the documents you have to go down there, in this [NON-ENGLISH] where all the Jews. That's where it started. I, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where the Jews were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew that they will go-- that-- you see, in Russia we didn't have any ghettos like here. You stay here and you work there, and that's it. No. They put them all in one place. And every night the truck comes. They put the people on the truck. They went, and we never saw them again. They-- they-- make the Jews to make the graves for themselves. And they're all gone. Yes. But I said, I'm going to get documents, and we're not going to this [NON-ENGLISH]. And he gave me three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, I left my mother [SOBBING] and sister down there. And I went by myself to Krasnodar, where we start, where I had my friends from university. And we left all those-- all our things, our possessions from our flat where I lived, to one family, which lived not far away. The grand piano and everything, they took it to their place. What I did, I even ask, we didn't look like-- I didn't look much like Jew. So I wasn't frightened of Germans. Germans were going by trucks from one-- from one town to another. So I was walking from the place I was to Krasnodar. But I was lucky. I was showing, and they took me on this. And because I knew a bit of German, they were thrilled to pieces I can-- German. So they put me on the truck, and I went. And this way I got to Krasnodar. I went to Krasnodar to this family where we got the-- What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They nearly had a heart attack in Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't remember. I can't-- I remember the names of the two, my friends, who did the documents for me. But I don't remember that family. So what we did, at night, they said, oh they had a shock. They were so frightened. I said, don't be frightened. I'm not staying here. I have to call to my friends. I know where they live. And they said-- I go to them. I have to have documents. So I will stay only till dark, and I will go there. So they will be relieved. Because everybody who give sort of hide for the people-- for Jewish people, wherever, you know, they can be shot the same as Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I-- I went at about 9 o'clock at night. I went to my friend. I went to-- Iakishina. And I told her the situation, that we stay down there. And we need some sort of documents. So we were talking with the parent-- with my mother and sister that we get name Chotowa. And they-- mom will be [NON-ENGLISH], and David [NON-ENGLISH]. I changed the names of all my-- only the first name like Liza, or Raissa, and [NON-ENGLISH] that was left. But names of parents and things, I change. So when I went to them, we have to have two witnesses. We went to a solicitor, and Iagunova, that's another girl who helped me, and they both went at night to the solicitor and they signed the documents. The solicitor wrote in Russia, that we Chotowa, this and that, lived in Krasnodar, and that he is from Krasnodar. He now resides and does our two signature. And that was good enough document to survive. So I took this document. And of course, with this document, I went back to my family, to my sisters. But that wasn't enough. I had to go to German authorities to get the same thing in German. So I went to German authorities. Gave them, this is my document. And I would like to have it translated in German. And they did it. So that's how we, that means me and my sisters and mother, stayed alive. We lived on those documents. If somebody came and asked, we had something to show that we're not a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mom was [NON-ENGLISH]. Because she was a bit Semitic so. We didn't know. But dad was on the front, he was in the army. So he was a Russian. My father Russian, and mom, [NON-ENGLISH]. That was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know how it's in English. But [NON-ENGLISH]. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you all went through the war then?

speaker: interviewee
text: As yes, as Chotowa, non-Jewish. Nobody knew that we're Jewish. Maybe somebody suspect us. But they couldn't do a thing. Because we have a document to prove it. That's how we survived. So that was the first. Now, when we-- when I had documents, I was gutsy enough to get to the underground organization too. By then we knew what Germans do. We knew he kills all Jews. He burns them. We knew everything what's going on. And of course, as a patriot, I want to sort of my-- my contribution to work against him, to get rid of him. So I start to work underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I started to work in Nikolaevka. So, to be able to do something, to get something, I applied for working in German kitchen. So that was very good. Because then I could help my family with food. Because whatever left over, I save scraps, I-- at night I took home. And we could eat. Right? But I knew things happening. I met there a person who was underground. And I-- he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only first name, Nikolai. Nikolai, that's his name. But that was all what I needed to know. And that's how we started in this. When-- when the Germans, when the Russians start to push, I had a command to go further to Ukraine. And I was moving from my mother and two sisters was left there in Nikolaevka. But I moved with this kitchen, where I was working, I was moved to Nikolaev. That was Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the kitchen supplying? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans, Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were moving around.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was-- they was staying with the-- how you call it? Army quarters, kitchen for the army quarters to-- there was hospital too, German hospital, where it was for German. Who was working for them? Cossack, Russians, whoever was there. They didn't work. The doctors were Germans. The-- the-- heads was Germans. But the workers was just people who live there. They give them work. So in Nikolaev, I had already a connection with one woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, and I don't want to remember. That's why I don't remember, most probably. Because she was the one that gave me away. And I was arrested, and put in Gestapo, thanks to her. It was in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back, going back just to the underground activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's the underground activities. So my-- my role in this organization was just to sort of-- to give people hope that Russia is coming. Because I come already where Germany was starting running. And I said, they come soon. We have to be prepared. We have to do our things, plus I have to provide ausweis. That's a document that gives the rights for a person to go at night out. It's usually for the partisan. Because the whole work for partisan was done at night. They couldn't move at night-- at daytime, only at night. And my-- I'm supposed to get some documents for them that gives him rights to walk around at nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get those?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's what happened. This girl that I was supposed to go, she's supposed to provide me with those documents. And I remember it was in May-- I don't know what date. It was in the beginning of May, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before you go on to that time, can you just go back a little bit to the resistance, and tell us what you were able to achieve?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we did is we-- we used to make picnics where I, and all others, people who believed in-- in that Russia will come. Because some of them didn't believe. Some of them was suicidal. Some of them good-- and some of them run. They went to Germans, you see. So what we did, we just put some papers, new papers. We had these. And I used to give everybody to put-- to give away for people to read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was on the paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the paper? We were don't-- don't-- don't lose your faith. We will be free, and do everything you can to help to free yourself. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your main job?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my job. I knew a few songs, [NON-ENGLISH], which I give them to sing and sing. But that was all such a-- we have to do it out of town. And it couldn't be done that often, because we didn't have the facilities. We have-- we didn't have-- we have to find a person that know where we can do it. And that I have to wait to let me know where I can do it. And it was rare, but that's what we did. So people start hoping that it will come time that Russia will be here. So that help Russian army too, to go and get them. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your period in the resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: In resistance, it was-- that was, I will tell you. I started in-- it started in January. [DRINKING]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. And it finishes May. That's approximately the time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to achieve the mission of getting these documents to people to be able to move around at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one. That was a new one. I had the other one from where I come from. There I got. But here I couldn't get it. See?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get it for other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? For other people I got where I was before. But I didn't get in Nikolaev. Because that was what happened, where it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you get the document help from in your other place?

speaker: interviewee
text: From a Gestapo. They have to give the permission to walk around at night, only Gestapo had the right. Nobody else can give you right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get them to give this-- these permits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Down there? Because I was working in the kitchen in German, and I had, you know, sort of-- they-- I had sort of-- they believed I'm German. So why not? And they gave me. They gave me once. Then they gave me another one. I said I've got a girl she's working there. So two, three, that was enough. You see, we couldn't get five, six, seven, 10 or whatever. But you have to have one or two or three or four. That was a big help. That's it. So in May, this girl that I was supposed to go with her to get in Gestapo, she was supposed to provide me with a job, because I didn't have yet the job, and to get me the documents. So I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you in at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nikolaev, Nikolaev. So we went in the morning, 10 o'clock or half past 9:00, something like this. And I walk, and all of a sudden, she was in the back. And I looked behind, and she was-- do some-- do her shoes or whatever. Well, I didn't pay attention. And I went around. And all of a sudden, I look up, and there's Gestapo. So I screamed to her, come here. And she got-- and this comes in, and he said, do you have a document that you work? I said, no, I'm going now to get the permission to work. He said, come in. He took me in. And because I called her, she come in. So they took her. We was-- that's when-- but as soon as we came in, they took me in one room, and they took her in another room. I still didn't know what's going on. I still thought that they need a document that I worked, but I didn't work. And I explained them that I'm going to get some job. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where it starts the nightmare. Of course, they didn't believe that I didn't get, and they start to asking me whom else you know, whom else you did. And they said, look, we know you're working for underground. I said, no. We know that you're working for underground. I said, I never worked. I don't know. Who told you? What if we have a person who will tell? I said, so she's a big liar, because I don't work. And that's what I did, confront with her. And I was screaming that she's a liar and I'm not. I'm not working. I don't know. I just come to get a job here. I couldn't find a job. And-- You did meet her through the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You did meet her through the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. But she's a liar, and I don't know her. I didn't have any way from then on, they start to interrogate me. Every night they took me, of course, that put me in the Gestapo cells. There was six cells. In one of those cells there was-- in every cell was about 10, 12 people. Every night they took me for questioning, three-- three gestapos was beating me with a-- how you got?

speaker: interviewer
text: A whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not wooden, but rubber, big rubber this. And, of course, by the time they finished with me, they brought me in this. I was blue and red with blood all over my body. And-- and-- [SOBBING] all the people was very sorry for me. But they couldn't do a thing. And because I insist that I don't know nothing, and that she's a liar. If she says so, no, no, no. They just put me again in the cell, and again in the cell. After about five days of interrogation and beating me, they just said, you're going to stay in this cell till we come to a conclusion what to do with you. So they put me in the cell. One thing that's in my mind, in the same cell, and next cells, and next cells, there was a lot of Jewish people. Every night they were called, [SOBBING] the truck was coming to take them away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At night, I feel-- I couldn't sleep for 30 days, as long I was there, because I was a frightened to scream out I'm a Jew too. [SOB] So I was frightened. I couldn't sleep. I was a frightened to sleep. As soon as I dream I was shaking like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was 30 days in Gestapo. Last time they took me down there, they called me in-- in head office, and told me that they give me a sentence of life in concentration camp. And I will be sent next day to the prison, and from prison to the concentration camp. And that's what happened. Next day, they took me to prison. I was in prison, I think a month. And from the prison, I was sent in concentration camp, Vodokachka. Vodokachka was not far from Nikolaev, maybe a few kilometers, I don't know, maybe 100 kilometers from Nikolaev.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was aproximately July?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was still [SIGHS] summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: July?

speaker: interviewee
text: July, about July.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the time that you were in the Gestapo being interrogated, were they interrogating you daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily, yes. If not physically, verbally. And one time they-- they screamed, you a Jew. And I said, no. And they knocked me, one SS come and knocked me with a big ring. This I've got still here. He said, you're a Jew. I said, no. So they said, repeat. [POINTING] [NON-ENGLISH], This is her-- she's going.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
